title: Tool Setup Guide: Java, Git, and GitHub Copilot
text:
This guide helps you get the required tools installed and opened for Week 1. After completing these checks, use Assignment 1 to practice with the tools, create your repository, capture screenshots, and complete the reflection.
The tool order below matches Assignment 1: JDK, IDE, Git/GitHub, Copilot, then a simple Java project.

title: Tool Setup Guide: Java, Git, and GitHub Copilot
text:
1. Install JDK 17+
Install a JDK from one of these sources:

Adoptium Temurin
Oracle Java Downloads

Confirm Java is available from a terminal:
java -version
You should see version 17 or newer.

title: Tool Setup Guide: Java, Git, and GitHub Copilot
text:
2. Install One IDE
Choose one development environment for the course. You do not need both.
Option A: IntelliJ IDEA

Download IntelliJ IDEA from JetBrains.
JetBrains recently merged the Community Edition into the main IntelliJ IDEA product offering. Students can also check the JetBrains Student Pack for access options.
Open IntelliJ and confirm it can create or open a Java project.

Option B: Visual Studio Code

Download VS Code from code.visualstudio.com.
Open Extensions (Cmd+Shift+X on macOS or Ctrl+Shift+X on Windows/Linux).
Install Extension Pack for Java.

Maven, Gradle, and JUnit are useful later in the course, but they are not required for the basic Assignment 1 setup check.

title: Tool Setup Guide: Java, Git, and GitHub Copilot
text:
3. Set Up Git and GitHub
Assignment 1 asks you to submit a Git repository link, so make sure Git and GitHub are ready before you start the assignment work.

Install Git from git-scm.com if it is not already installed.
Confirm Git is available:

git --version

Sign in to GitHub or create an account.
Set up GitHub Education using your university email address at github.com/settings/education/benefits. This is how students access GitHub Education benefits, including Copilot access when approved.
Confirm you know how you will connect to GitHub: HTTPS or SSH. GitHub’s docs explain both options in Set up Git.

title: Tool Setup Guide: Java, Git, and GitHub Copilot
text:
4. Enable GitHub Copilot
GitHub Copilot is the only AI assistant approved for Assignment 1. Before installing the IDE plugin, request or confirm GitHub Education benefits with your university email address at github.com/settings/education/benefits.
In IntelliJ IDEA

Open Settings/Preferences > Plugins.
Search for GitHub Copilot in the Marketplace and install it.
Restart IntelliJ if prompted.
Sign in with your GitHub account when prompted.

In VS Code

Open Extensions.
Install GitHub Copilot.
Sign in with your GitHub account when prompted.

If you need access help, see GitHub Copilot documentation.

title: Tool Setup Guide: Java, Git, and GitHub Copilot
Tool Setup Guide: Java, Git, and GitHub Copilot
text:
6. Confirm Copilot Suggestions Appear
In Main.java, type a short comment such as:
// method to add two numbers
Pause briefly and look for Copilot ghost text. If a suggestion appears, accept it with Tab or your IDE’s configured accept-suggestion shortcut.
Do not spend time making this code complex. The goal is only to confirm that Java, your IDE, GitHub Copilot, and GitHub are ready.

title: Tool Setup Guide: Java, Git, and GitHub Copilot
text:
7. Continue in Assignment 1
This setup guide stops once the tools are working. Complete the actual Week 1 deliverables in Assignment 1: Environment Setup & AI-Assisted SWE Foundations.
Assignment 1 is where you will:

Create the Git repository.
Commit the minimal Java project.
Add one Copilot-generated method.
Add a README.md with setup confirmation.
Capture screenshots.
Complete the conceptual reflection.

title: Tool Setup Guide: Java, Git, and GitHub Copilot
text:
Need Help?

Java in VS Code
IntelliJ IDEA documentation
GitHub Docs: Set up Git
GitHub Copilot documentation
Ask questions in your course forum or contact your instructor.

title: COMP 331: AI-Assisted Software Engineering Orientation
text: Quick Links: - Syllabus & Weekly Schedule - Tool Setup Guide

title: COMP 331: AI-Assisted Software Engineering Orientation
text:
1) Welcome & Course Introduction
Instructor: Add name, office hours, contact preferences
Format: 12-week · 4 credits · Prerequisite: COMP 311 | Advanced Java Programming (or equivalent)
Why this course now?
AI-assisted development is changing how software is built. You’ll learn to pair your expertise with tools like GitHub Copilot to generate code, tests, docs, and plans—while critically evaluating quality, security, and ethics.
Course arc (high-level):

Week 1: Environment setup and AI-assisted software engineering foundations
Weeks 2–10: Weekly AI Interaction Log documenting prompts, decisions, and evaluations
Weeks 3, 5, 7, 9, and 10: Labs that build a Controlled Coding Agent workflow
Weeks 10–12: Final Project proposal, partial implementation, evaluation strategy, risk analysis, final system, and demo

title: COMP 331: AI-Assisted Software Engineering Orientation
text:
2) Syllabus & Learning Outcomes
See the Syllabus & Weekly Schedule for full course description, learning outcomes, topics, grading breakdown, and weekly activities.

Catalog Description
Explores how software engineers can use AI-assisted tools intentionally while building controlled, evaluable coding-agent workflows. Emphasis is placed on structured task input, AI-assisted code generation, evaluation, risk analysis, human oversight, reproducibility, and professional documentation. Hands-on labs build toward a final Controlled Coding Agent project in Java.
Learning Outcomes (mapped to Bloom’s levels):

Set up and verify a Java development environment with GitHub Copilot and GitHub-based submission workflows. (Apply)
Document AI-assisted development decisions through weekly interaction logs. (Analyze)
Translate feature requests into structured task specifications for AI-assisted implementation. (Create)
Define and run evaluations that measure correctness and detect regressions in AI-generated outputs. (Evaluate)
Integrate structured input, AI-assisted generation, evaluation, and human approval into a controlled coding-agent workflow. (Create)
Analyze reliability, security, maintainability, and production-readiness risks in AI-assisted systems. (Analyze/Evaluate)
Deliver a complete Controlled Coding Agent final project with documentation, evaluation evidence, and a reproducible demo. (Create)

title: COMP 331: AI-Assisted Software Engineering Orientation
text:
3) Tools & Setup (Week 1 Assignment)
See the Tool Setup Guide for step-by-step instructions for installing and configuring your IDE, Java, GitHub Copilot, and related tools.

Required Software/Accounts - JDK (latest LTS) - IntelliJ IDEA or Visual Studio Code - Git + GitHub account - GitHub Copilot with IDE plugin; students should set up GitHub Education using their university email address at github.com/settings/education/benefits - Maven, Gradle, and JUnit 5 are introduced later for labs and evaluations
Setup Checklist - [ ] Install JDK and confirm java -version - [ ] Install IntelliJ or VS Code - [ ] Install Git and configure SSH/HTTPS for GitHub - [ ] Set up GitHub Education with your university email address - [ ] Enable GitHub Copilot and sign in from IDE - [ ] Create a sample Java project; generate a simple method using Copilot - [ ] Confirm a simple Java project runs
Submission (Assignment 1): - Screenshots of IDE + Copilot in action - Brief reflection (150–250 words) on setup experience and Week 1 concepts

title: COMP 331: AI-Assisted Software Engineering Orientation
text:
4) Course Logistics
See the Syllabus & Weekly Schedule for details on modality, workload, assessment weights, deadlines, and submission conventions.

Modality & Rhythm - Weekly cadence: lecture/discussion (concepts), live demo, guided lab, short reflection - Estimated workload: 8–10 hours/week
Assessments & Weights - Assignment 1: Environment Setup & AI-Assisted SWE Foundations: 30 points - Weekly AI Interaction Log: 90 points - Labs 1–5: 480 points total, building the Controlled Coding Agent workflow over time - Final Project Milestones and Final Submission: 400 points total - Total: 1000 points
Deadlines & Late Policy - Standard deadline: end of week (Sunday 11:59 PM local) - Extensions for documented circumstances—contact instructor early per university policies
Submission Conventions - Code via GitHub; submit Canvas link, repository link, and commit hash when requested - Unless your professor instructs otherwise, continue extending the same project repository through Labs 1–5 and the Final Project - Include README.md with how to run, how to evaluate, and AI usage notes - Maintain weekly AI interaction log entries from Week 2 through Week 10, including Copilot mode, accepted/rejected suggestions, and verification method - Final submissions must include reproducible setup/run instructions and a demo link when required

title: COMP 331: AI-Assisted Software Engineering Orientation
section: 5) AI Use, Evidence, and Academic Integrity
text:
5) AI Use, Evidence, and Academic Integrity
GitHub Copilot is the approved AI assistant for course programming work unless an assignment says otherwise. You may use Copilot to help draft code, tests, documentation, evaluation criteria, and workflow artifacts, but you remain responsible for every submitted artifact.
Required Evidence

Record meaningful Copilot interactions in the Weekly AI Interaction Log.
Identify the Copilot mode used, such as inline suggestion, chat, edit, agent/workspace, test generation, review, or debugging.
Describe AI assistance in each repository README or assignment documentation.
Preserve prompts, accepted or rejected suggestions, manual edits, evaluations, verification methods, and final decisions when relevant.

Not Allowed

Submitting AI-generated work without acknowledgment.
Submitting code you do not understand or cannot explain.
Sharing full solutions with classmates unless the professor explicitly makes an activity collaborative.
Pasting secrets, private data, proprietary code, or protected information into AI tools.

title: COMP 331: AI-Assisted Software Engineering Orientation
section: 6) Risks To Watch Throughout the Course
text:
6) Risks To Watch Throughout the Course
The labs and Final Project ask you to evaluate AI-assisted systems, not just produce code. Keep these risks visible in your weekly logs, evaluations, and project documentation.

Correctness: Generated code may appear plausible while missing edge cases or requirements.
Security: AI suggestions may introduce unsafe input handling, secret leakage, weak cryptography, or injection risks.
Maintainability: Generated code may be hard to read, overcomplicated, or poorly aligned with your architecture.
Evaluation gaps: Passing one demo does not prove the workflow is reliable. Define measurable checks.
Human oversight: Controlled workflows need clear points where a human reviews, approves, rejects, or revises AI output.

title: COMP 331: AI-Assisted Software Engineering Orientation
text:
7) Learning Activities
Controlled Workflow Pattern

Specify: Translate a request into structured input with goals, constraints, edge cases, and acceptance criteria
Generate or revise: Use Copilot inline suggestions, Chat, edit/review features, or agent-style assistance to propose implementation, tests, documentation, or workflow artifacts
Evaluate: Run tests, compiler checks, scripts, manual reviews, or structured comparisons against explicit criteria
Control: Add human approval checkpoints before accepting important changes
Document: Preserve prompts, outputs, evaluation results, risks, and final decisions

This pattern appears repeatedly in Labs 1-5 and the Final Project. The goal is not to maximize AI usage; the goal is to make AI-assisted development controlled, observable, evaluable, and reproducible.

title: COMP 331: AI-Assisted Software Engineering Orientation
text:
8) Success Strategies

Keep the Weekly AI Interaction Log current: record Copilot mode, prompts, outputs, verification methods, decisions, and what worked
Prefer small, precise prompts over broad requests; iterate quickly
Define evaluation criteria before trusting generated code; use AI to scaffold edge cases where appropriate
Use tests, static analysis, security checks, and manual review alongside AI suggestions
Commit early/often; preserve artifacts that make workflow decisions reproducible
Treat AI like a junior pair-programmer: useful for drafts, but never the final authority

title: COMP 331: AI-Assisted Software Engineering Orientation
text:
9) Orientation Activities (Week 1)
Icebreaker (Forum): Share your first experience using AI on codewhat surprised you?
Mini-Demo (in class or video): Instructor demonstrates Copilot in a small Java file, then critiques the output for intent match, correctness, and risk.
Quick Poll: Which part of a controlled AI workflow seems most challenging? Options: specifying tasks, generating code, evaluating outputs, managing risk, documenting decisions
Checklist TurnIn: Confirm tools installed; Copilot active; simple Java project running.

title: COMP 331: AI-Assisted Software Engineering Orientation
text:
10) Week 1 Deliverables

Assignment 1: Setup verification + short reflection
Weekly AI Interaction Log: Begins in Week 2 and continues through Week 10

Rubric Snapshot (Assignment 1)

Environment, GitHub, and Copilot setup completed — 10 points
Screenshots clearly demonstrate setup — 5 points
Repository includes minimal Java project and README — 5 points
README documents responsible Copilot use and verification — 5 points
Reflection demonstrates understanding of Week 1 concepts — 5 points

title: COMP 331: AI-Assisted Software Engineering Orientation
text:
11) Quick Reference & Links (fill in LMS)

Course site / Canvas module: link
GitHub organization / assignment repository: link
Weekly AI Interaction Log location: link
Final Project repository: link
GitHub Education setup: github.com/settings/education/benefits
Copilot documentation: link
Java/JUnit templates, evaluation examples, and build scripts (Maven/Gradle): links
Office hours / booking link: link
Communication channel (Discord/Slack/Ed): link

Contact Protocol

For bugs or repo issues — open a GitHub Issue
For grading/logistics — course forum or email
For private matters — office hours or direct message

title: Lab 1: Structured AI Workflow
text:
Course Name/Number: COMP 331: AI-Assisted Software Engineering
Week: 3
Points: 90
Continue maintaining the Weekly AI Interaction Log while completing this lab.

title: Lab 1: Structured AI Workflow
text:
1 Purpose of Assignment
This assignment is intended to help you:

Establish the task input layer of your coding agent
Translate feature requests into structured specifications
Compare naive vs structured AI-assisted development
Begin building the foundation of a Java-based coding agent system

title: Lab 1: Structured AI Workflow
text:
2 Steps / Requirements of Assignment
Build the first layer of a Controlled Coding Agent workflow: a Java command-line program that can accept a user request, send it to an LLM, and compare naive prompting with structured prompting. This lab should feel small and controlled. You are not building the full agent yet.
Start fresh for this lab. Create a new repository named comp331-java-agent. Do not use your Assignment 1 setup repository as the starting point unless your professor explicitly approves it. This Lab 1 repository should become your continuing repository for Labs 1-5 and may become the starting point for your final project.

Create a fresh GitHub repository named comp331-java-agent.
Create a Java project using Maven in that repository. A simple structure such as src/main/java/.../Main.java is enough.
Configure your Gemini API key using the Gemini API Setup Guide. Do not commit API keys, .env files, or other secrets.
Write a Main class that first sends one hard-coded prompt to the model and prints the response. This verifies that your Java project can connect to the API.
Revise the program so it accepts the user prompt from command-line arguments, for example mvn exec:java -Dexec.args="Explain this Java method".
Add a simple message representation for later labs. This may be a small Message class or record with fields such as role and content.
Add a --verbose flag or similar option that prints helpful debugging information, such as the user prompt, selected model, and token counts if your API provides them.
Choose a small Java feature request that can be implemented in a utility class, such as formatting a string, validating input, parsing a simple expression, or computing a value.
Ask GitHub Copilot or another approved coding assistant for help once using a naive prompt or informal comment. Save the prompt and resulting code or describe the interaction.
Create a structured task specification with goal, inputs, outputs, constraints, edge cases, and acceptance criteria. Use that specification to revise or re-implement the feature.
Compare the naive and structured approaches in your README. Focus on concrete differences in output quality, missing edge cases, and human decisions.

Suggested checkpoint commands:
mvn test
mvn exec:java -Dexec.args="Why are structured prompts useful for Java development?"
mvn exec:java -Dexec.args="--verbose Explain what this program does"

title: Lab 1: Structured AI Workflow
text:
3 Action Items

Click the link to the full hosted lab instructions: Lab 1: Structured AI Workflow.
Follow the instructions in the lab page.
Go to github.com and create a fresh repository named comp331-java-agent.
Build the Java LLM command-line client and naive vs structured Copilot comparison described in the linked instructions.
Document setup, run commands, prompts, accepted/rejected Copilot suggestions, comparison notes, and verification results in README.md.
Copy meaningful prompts and Copilot interaction notes into your weekly AI interaction reflection.
Submit the repository link through Canvas.

title: Lab 1: Structured AI Workflow
text:
4 Using GitHub Copilot
Use GitHub Copilot to compare unstructured assistance with intentional, structured assistance. You remain responsible for understanding, testing, and explaining all submitted work.
Required Copilot workflow:

Use inline Copilot once with a vague comment or naive prompt. Save the prompt/comment and result.
Use Copilot Chat again with a structured task specification. Include goal, inputs, outputs, constraints, edge cases, and acceptance criteria.
Ask Copilot to explain the structured result before accepting it.
Compare the naive and structured outputs in your README. Focus on correctness, missing edge cases, and what you changed manually.

Suggested structured prompt:
You are helping me implement a small Java utility method.
Goal:
Inputs:
Outputs:
Constraints:
Edge cases:
Acceptance criteria:
Generate only the method and a short explanation.

title: Lab 1: Structured AI Workflow
text:
5 Deliverables
Your repository should include:

A fresh Lab 1 repository named comp331-java-agent.
A runnable Java Maven project with a command-line entry point.
Code that sends a prompt to an LLM from Java.
Command-line prompt handling and a verbose/debug mode.
Java source code for both the naive and structured feature attempts, or clearly separated commits showing each approach.
A structured task specification.
A README.md explaining setup, API key configuration, run commands, and the naive vs structured comparison.
Copilot evidence showing the naive interaction, structured interaction, accepted changes, rejected or revised suggestions, and verification method.
Evidence of AI-assisted development through prompts, comments, screenshots, transcript snippets, or log links.

title: Lab 1: Structured AI Workflow
text:
6 Grading







Category
Points




Java LLM CLI is runnable and documented
25


Structured task specification is clear and complete
20


Naive and structured implementations are both present
20


Comparison explains concrete differences in AI outputs and human decisions
15


Copilot use is documented with accepted, revised, and verified suggestions
10


Fresh comp331-java-agent repository is organized with README setup and run instructions
5


Verbose/debug mode or equivalent inspection support is included
5


Secret handling is appropriate
5


Total
90

title: Lab 1: Structured AI Workflow
text:
7 Submission Instructions
Share the comp331-java-agent Git repository with your professor per their instructions and submit the repository link through Canvas. Include any setup notes needed to run your Java project.
Next lab: Lab 2: Eval-Driven Development.

title: Final Project Milestone 2: Partial Implementation, Evaluation Strategy, and Identified Risks
text:
Course Name/Number: COMP 331: AI-Assisted Software Engineering
Week: 11
Points: 140
This milestone extends Final Project Milestone 1.

title: Final Project Milestone 2: Partial Implementation, Evaluation Strategy, and Identified Risks
text:
1 Purpose of Assignment
This assignment is intended to help you:

Demonstrate meaningful progress on your coding agent system
Define and implement an evaluation strategy
Identify risks and limitations in your system
Prepare for final system completion

title: Final Project Milestone 2: Partial Implementation, Evaluation Strategy, and Identified Risks
text:
2 Steps / Requirements of Assignment
Submit a partial implementation that demonstrates the core direction of your final project. The system does not need to be complete, but it must show meaningful progress and a concrete evaluation strategy.

Implement the most important workflow path or system component.
Add or refine the evaluation strategy.
Run the current system on at least one example task.
Identify current risks, limitations, and incomplete pieces.
Update your plan for the final submission.

title: Final Project Milestone 2: Partial Implementation, Evaluation Strategy, and Identified Risks
text:
3 Using GitHub Copilot
Use GitHub Copilot for focused implementation, debugging, and evaluation work. Do not ask Copilot to generate the whole project at once; use it on scoped components and verify each result.
Recommended Copilot workflow:

Use Copilot for one focused implementation task tied to your architecture.
Use Copilot to improve or expand evaluation cases.
Use Copilot to debug one failing example run, then document the fix and verification.
Identify which implementation pieces were Copilot-assisted and how each was verified.

title: Final Project Milestone 2: Partial Implementation, Evaluation Strategy, and Identified Risks
text:
4 Action Items

Click the link to the full hosted milestone instructions: Final Project Milestone 2.
Follow the instructions in the milestone page.
Open your final project repository and review the proposal, architecture, and milestone checklist from Milestone 1.
Complete the partial implementation, evaluation strategy, example run, risk update, and final completion plan described in the linked instructions.
Add Copilot-assisted implementation notes identifying assisted pieces, accepted suggestions, rejected or modified suggestions, and verification methods.
Submit the repository link, current runnable path, and known setup constraints through Canvas.

title: Final Project Milestone 2: Partial Implementation, Evaluation Strategy, and Identified Risks
text:
5 Deliverables
Your repository should include:

Partial implementation of the Controlled Coding Agent.
Evaluation strategy and current evaluation artifacts.
Example run or walkthrough.
Risk and limitations section.
Copilot-assisted implementation notes identifying assisted pieces and verification methods.
Updated final completion plan.

title: Final Project Milestone 2: Partial Implementation, Evaluation Strategy, and Identified Risks
text:
6 Grading







Category
Points




Partial implementation shows meaningful progress
45


Evaluation strategy is defined and partially implemented
35


Example run demonstrates current workflow
20


Risks, limitations, and next steps are clearly documented
20


Copilot-assisted implementation evidence is specific and verified
5


Repository remains organized and usable
15


Total
140

title: Final Project Milestone 2: Partial Implementation, Evaluation Strategy, and Identified Risks
text:
7 Submission Instructions
Submit your project repository link through Canvas. Identify the current runnable path, any known setup constraints, and where to find Copilot-assisted implementation notes.
Next: Final Project Final Submission.

title: Weekly AI Interaction Log
text:
Course Name/Number: COMP 331: AI-Assisted Software Engineering
Weeks: 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9, 10
Points: 10 points each week / 90 total

title: Weekly AI Interaction Log
text:
1 Purpose of Assignment
This assignment is intended to help you learn to do the following:

Develop intentional and structured use of GitHub Copilot or another approved AI/LLM tool
Reflect on how prompting, iteration, and evaluation affect outcomes
Identify patterns in successful and unsuccessful AI interactions
Build awareness of your own engineering decision-making process
Document how AI-assisted development evolves over time

title: Weekly AI Interaction Log
text:
2 Steps / Requirements of Assignment
Submit a separate weekly AI interaction reflection from Week 2 through Week 10. Each week, create and submit a new document that contains only that week’s log entry. Do not submit one cumulative document containing multiple weeks unless your professor explicitly asks for it. Each weekly reflection should be submitted through Canvas as a document or PDF, such as .docx or .pdf, unless your professor approves another format.
Each weekly entry should include:

Week number and date
Development task or learning activity
Your intent before using Copilot
Copilot mode used: inline suggestion, chat, edit, agent/workspace, test generation, review, or debugging
Prompt, comment, or interaction summary
AI tool output or suggestion summary
What you accepted, modified, or rejected
Verification method: test, compiler, manual review, comparison, or rejected output
Reflection on what worked, what failed, and what you will change next time

Suggested template:
Intent:
Copilot mode used:
Context I gave Copilot:
Copilot output:
What I accepted:
What I changed:
How I verified it:
What I learned:

title: Weekly AI Interaction Log
text:
3 Action Items

Click the link to the full hosted assignment instructions: Weekly AI Interaction Log.
Follow the instructions in the assignment page.
Go to your preferred document editor, such as Google Docs at docs.google.com, Microsoft Word, or another professor-approved format.
Each week, create a new document for that week only. Do not submit a cumulative log with previous weeks included.
Use GitHub Copilot as directed in the linked instructions and copy meaningful prompts, outputs, accepted/rejected suggestions, and verification notes into the log.
Write the required reflection paragraph about your initial prompt, prompt iterations, successful prompt patterns, unsuccessful prompt patterns, and what you will change next time.
Export or save the current week’s entry as a document or PDF and submit it through Canvas.

title: Weekly AI Interaction Log
text:
4 Deliverables
Submit only the current week’s reflection as a separate document or PDF through Canvas. The submitted file should contain that week’s log entry only, not a cumulative log from multiple weeks. A repository link is not required for this weekly reflection unless your professor specifically asks for one. Do not rely on lab repository notes as your weekly submission unless your professor explicitly approves that format.
Suggested filename format:
week02-ai-reflection-yourname.pdf

title: Weekly AI Interaction Log
text:
5 Grading
Each weekly entry is worth 10 points.



Category
Points




Clear task context and intent
2


Copilot mode and interaction documented with enough specificity
3


Verification and human decision-making explained
3


Reflection identifies a useful pattern, risk, or next step
2


Weekly Total
10



Across Weeks 2-10, the assignment is worth 90 points total.

title: Weekly AI Interaction Log
text:
6 Submission Instructions
Submit the current week’s reflection document or PDF through Canvas. Each weekly submission should be a separate file containing only that week’s log entry. Follow any additional instructions provided by your professor.

title: Lab 2: Eval-Driven Development
text:
Course Name/Number: COMP 331: AI-Assisted Software Engineering
Week: 5
Points: 100
This lab extends Lab 1: Structured AI Workflow. Continue maintaining the Weekly AI Interaction Log.

title: Lab 2: Eval-Driven Development
text:
1 Purpose of Assignment
This assignment is intended to help you:

Build the evaluation layer of your coding agent
Define measurable correctness criteria
Detect regressions in AI-generated outputs
Create the Java sandbox project that later agent tools will inspect and modify

title: Lab 2: Eval-Driven Development
text:
2 Steps / Requirements of Assignment
Extend your Lab 1 repository by adding a small Java sandbox project and a repeatable evaluation process. Later labs will give the coding agent tools that operate on this sandbox, so keep it simple and easy to test.

Create a sandbox Java project or package inside your repository. A recommended name is workspace/calculator or sandbox/calculator.
Implement a small Java program or library that has clear behavior. Recommended options include a calculator, string formatter, unit converter, password validator, or CSV summary utility.
Add JUnit tests that check normal cases, edge cases, and at least one invalid-input case.
Define measurable acceptance criteria in your README. Each criterion should connect to a test, command, or review check.
Run the evaluation manually from the command line using mvn test or an equivalent command. Save the output or summarize it in your README.
Use Copilot or another approved AI tool to make at least two iterations of the sandbox code. One iteration may intentionally be incomplete or flawed.
Run the evaluation against at least two versions or iterations of the code and document what changed. You may use separate commits, branches, screenshots, copied terminal output, or a written comparison.
Connect the evaluation back to your Lab 1 structured prompt/specification. Note which acceptance criteria were enforced automatically and which still required human review.

Suggested checkpoint commands:
mvn test
mvn -q test
git log --oneline
Minimum viable version: one sandbox Java class and at least five meaningful JUnit tests. Stretch goal: add a small script or Maven profile that runs only the sandbox evaluation.

title: Lab 2: Eval-Driven Development
text:
3 Action Items

Click the link to the full hosted lab instructions: Lab 2: Eval-Driven Development.
Follow the instructions in the lab page.
Open your existing comp331-java-agent repository from Lab 1.
Create the sandbox project, JUnit evaluation, and Copilot-assisted iterations described in the linked instructions.
Document acceptance criteria, test commands, iteration results, Copilot-assisted test or code changes, and verification results in README.md.
Copy meaningful Copilot prompts, test-generation notes, and evaluation decisions into your weekly AI interaction reflection.
Submit the repository link and required evaluation evidence through Canvas.

title: Lab 2: Eval-Driven Development
text:
4 Using GitHub Copilot
Use GitHub Copilot to strengthen the evaluation process, not to bypass it. You remain responsible for deciding which tests matter and whether the implementation satisfies the assignment.
Recommended Copilot workflow:

Ask Copilot Chat to propose JUnit tests before implementation. Review the suggestions and keep only meaningful cases.
Ask Copilot to identify missing edge cases after your first test set is written.
Use Copilot to help create one incomplete or flawed implementation, then use your tests to expose the weakness.
Label tests or test ideas as human-authored, Copilot-assisted, or Copilot-suggested but modified.
Record how each accepted Copilot suggestion was verified with mvn test or an equivalent command.

title: Lab 2: Eval-Driven Development
text:
5 Deliverables
Your repository should include:

A Java sandbox project or package that later labs can inspect and modify.
Evaluation criteria in a documented format.
Runnable JUnit tests or an equivalent automated evaluation command.

title: Lab 3: AI System Implementation
text:
Course Name/Number: COMP 331: AI-Assisted Software Engineering
Week: 7
Points: 100
This lab extends Lab 2: Eval-Driven Development. Continue maintaining the Weekly AI Interaction Log.

title: Lab 3: AI System Implementation
text:
1 Purpose of Assignment
This assignment is intended to help you:

Integrate components into a working coding agent workflow
Connect structured input, AI generation, and evaluation
Transition from isolated tasks to a cohesive system
Implement safe Java tools that allow an agent to inspect and work inside a sandbox

title: Lab 3: AI System Implementation
text:
2 Steps / Requirements of Assignment
Integrate your structured input and evaluation work by adding Java tool implementations. These tools are the controlled operations your coding agent will later expose to the LLM. At this stage, you may test the tools directly from Java code or JUnit; the model does not need to call them automatically yet.

Define a common tool interface or base pattern. For example, you might create an AgentTool interface with methods such as name(), description(), and execute(...).
Define one sandbox working directory. This should point to the Java sandbox project from Lab 2, such as workspace/calculator.
Implement a file listing tool similar to get_files_info. It should list files and directories plus basic metadata such as size and whether each entry is a directory.
Implement a file reading tool similar to get_file_content. It should read text files from the sandbox and cap output at a reasonable length, such as 10,000 characters.
Implement a file writing tool similar to write_file. It should create or overwrite files only inside the sandbox.
Implement a command execution tool similar to run_python_file, adapted for Java. Recommended: RunJavaTestsTool that runs mvn test or gradle test in the sandbox. Avoid arbitrary shell access unless you have a strict allowlist.
Use Java Path APIs to prevent path traversal. Paths such as ../README.md or absolute paths outside the sandbox must be rejected.
Add JUnit tests for each tool. Include successful cases and blocked cases for missing files, invalid directories, path traversal, and command restrictions.
Add a small manual driver or test command that demonstrates the tools working together: list files, read a Java file, write a harmless file, and run the sandbox tests.
Document the architecture and current limitations in your README.

Suggested Java path-safety approach:
Path sandbox = Path.of("workspace/calculator").toAbsolutePath().normalize();
Path target = sandbox.resolve(userProvidedPath).normalize();
if (!target.startsWith(sandbox)) {
    throw new IllegalArgumentException("Path is outside the sandbox");
}
Suggested checkpoint commands:
mvn test
mvn exec:java -Dexec.args="--tool-demo"

title: Lab 3: AI System Implementation
text:
3 Action Items

Follow the instructions in the lab page.

Implement the Java tool layer, sandbox restrictions, tool tests, and demo described in the linked instructions.
Document the tool architecture, sample run, accepted/rejected Copilot suggestions, and verification results in README.md.
Copy meaningful prompts, iterations, and evaluation outcomes into your weekly AI interaction reflection.
Submit the repository link and required tool demo or test evidence through Canvas.

title: Lab 3: AI System Implementation
text:
4 Using GitHub Copilot
Use GitHub Copilot as a design and review assistant for the tool layer. Be especially cautious with file and command tools; Copilot may suggest implementations that are too permissive.
Recommended Copilot workflow:

Ask Copilot Chat to brainstorm a small Java interface for tools before coding.
Use Copilot to draft tests for path traversal, missing files, blocked absolute paths, and command allowlists.
Ask Copilot to review your implementation for sandbox escape risks.
Document at least one Copilot suggestion you rejected because it was unsafe, overbroad, or insufficiently tested.
Verify accepted suggestions with JUnit tests and the suggested checkpoint commands.

title: Lab 3: AI System Implementation
text:
5 Deliverables
Your repository should include:

Working Java source code for the agent tool layer.
Tools for listing files, reading files, writing files, and running Java evaluation commands.
Sandbox path validation that blocks access outside the permitted working directory.
JUnit tests for successful and blocked tool behavior.
Architecture documentation in the README or a separate design document.
A sample tool demo or transcript showing at least one successful workflow.
Evaluation results from at least one sandbox test run.
Copilot notes describing one accepted suggestion, one rejected or revised suggestion, and the verification used.

100

title: Assignment 1: Environment Setup & AI-Assisted SWE Foundations
text:
1 Purpose of Assignment
This assignment is intended to help you:

Set up your development environment for AI-assisted software engineering
Verify GitHub Copilot is working correctly
Understand the difference between vibe coding and structured AI-assisted development
Introduce the concept of programming with intent
Recognize key strengths and limitations of AI-generated code

title: Assignment 1: Environment Setup & AI-Assisted SWE Foundations
text:
2 Steps / Requirements of Assignment
Complete the following setup tasks:

Install JDK 17+ and verify installation with:

java -version

Install one development environment:

IntelliJ IDEA, or
VS Code with Java extensions

Install Git, sign in to GitHub, set up GitHub Education using your university email address at github.com/settings/education/benefits, and confirm you can create or access a GitHub repository.
Install and sign in to GitHub Copilot.
Create a separate GitHub repository for this assignment named:

comp331-assignment1-setup
This repository is only for Assignment 1 setup verification. You will create a fresh repository for Lab 1 and do not need to reuse this setup repository for later labs.

In that repository, create a simple Java project and add a basic Main.java file.
Verify Copilot suggestions are working:

Type a simple comment such as:


// method to add two numbers

Confirm Copilot ghost text appears.
Accept at least one suggestion.


Generate one simple method using Copilot.

Examples: - Add two numbers - Reverse a string - Check if a number is even
Keep the code simple. The goal is to confirm your setup works.

title: Assignment 1: Environment Setup & AI-Assisted SWE Foundations
text:
3 Conceptual Reflection
Write a short reflection of 150–250 words addressing the following:

What is the difference between vibe coding and AI-assisted software engineering?
What does programming with intent mean in practice?
What are key strengths and limitations of AI-generated code?
How do you interpret the Software 2.0 perspective, and what are its implications for software development?

Your response should be concise, thoughtful, and written in your own words.

title: Assignment 1: Environment Setup & AI-Assisted SWE Foundations
text:
4 Using GitHub Copilot
You are encouraged to use GitHub Copilot in this assignment, but you remain responsible for understanding and explaining all submitted work. Do not accept suggestions blindly.
Use Copilot in small, observable ways:

Use inline suggestions from a simple Java comment, such as // method to reverse a string.
Use Copilot Chat for setup questions, such as How do I create a minimal Maven Java project?.
Ask Copilot to explain generated code before you accept it.
Document one Copilot suggestion you accepted and one change you made manually.

Add a short README.md note describing how you used Copilot and how you confirmed the generated method works.

title: Lab 4: AI Risk Analysis and Production Readiness
text:
Course Name/Number: COMP 331: AI-Assisted Software Engineering
Week: 9
Points: 90
This lab analyzes the system built in Lab 3: AI System Implementation. Continue maintaining the Weekly AI Interaction Log.

title: Lab 4: AI Risk Analysis and Production Readiness
text:
1 Purpose of Assignment
This assignment is intended to help you:

Analyze failure modes in your coding agent
Evaluate reliability, security, and maintainability
Propose improvements to increase robustness
Strengthen the safety controls around Java tools before adding an autonomous loop

title: Lab 4: AI Risk Analysis and Production Readiness
text:
2 Steps / Requirements of Assignment
Conduct a risk analysis of your current Java coding agent workflow. Focus on realistic failures in your own implementation rather than generic AI risks. Your system now has tools that can inspect files, read files, write files, and run Java evaluation commands, so your analysis should be specific to those capabilities.

Review each tool from Lab 3 and identify what could go wrong if an LLM requested unsafe or incorrect arguments.
Identify at least six failure modes. Include path traversal, accidental overwrite, command execution risk, secret exposure, misleading model output, incomplete tests, and human oversight gaps where relevant.
Analyze reliability, security, maintainability, and human oversight risks. At least one risk must come from each category.
Rate each risk by likelihood and impact using a simple scale such as Low, Medium, or High.
Propose concrete mitigations. Examples include path normalization, read-size limits, command allowlists, write approval checkpoints, max iteration limits, logging, and better test coverage.
Implement at least two small safeguards or tests based on your analysis. Reasonable options include blocking absolute paths, adding a command allowlist, adding a write confirmation mode, or adding tests for secret-like files.
Add a production-readiness checklist that distinguishes what is currently safe enough for coursework from what would be required before real use.
Identify which mitigations you plan to carry into Lab 5 and the final project.

Suggested risk table columns:












Risk
Tool/Component
Category
Likelihood
Impact
Current Control
Proposed Mitigation





Suggested checkpoint commands:
mvn test
mvn test -Dtest=*Tool*

title: Lab 4: AI Risk Analysis and Production Readiness
text:
3 Action Items

Click the link to the full hosted lab instructions: Lab 4: AI Risk Analysis and Production Readiness.
Follow the instructions in the lab page.
Open your existing comp331-java-agent repository and review each Lab 3 tool implementation.
Complete the risk analysis, safeguard implementation, and safety-test work described in the linked instructions.
Document the risk table, production-readiness checklist, prioritized improvement plan, Copilot-assisted analysis notes, and verification results in the repository.
Copy meaningful Copilot risk-analysis prompts and verification decisions into your weekly AI interaction reflection.
Submit the repository link and risk-analysis document through Canvas.

title: Lab 4: AI Risk Analysis and Production Readiness
text:
4 Using GitHub Copilot
Use GitHub Copilot as a risk-analysis assistant, not as the final authority. Your risk analysis must be specific to your own Java tool implementation.
Recommended Copilot workflow:

Ask Copilot to identify failure modes for your actual file, path, write, and test-execution tools.
Verify Copilot’s suggestions against your code and tests. Remove generic risks that do not apply.
Ask Copilot to critique your proposed mitigations, then decide which safeguards are realistic for this project.
Include one example where Copilot missed, understated, or overstated a risk.

Suggested prompt:
Review this Java coding-agent tool design for realistic risks.
Focus on reliability, security, maintainability, and human oversight.
Identify concrete failure modes tied to file reading, file writing, path handling, and test execution.
Do not give generic AI risk language.

title: Lab 4: AI Risk Analysis and Production Readiness
text:
5 Deliverables
Your repository should include:

Risk analysis document or README section specific to your Java coding agent.
Failure mode table with likelihood, impact, current controls, and mitigation.
Evidence of at least two implemented safeguards or safety tests.
Production-readiness checklist.
Prioritized improvement plan for Lab 5 and the final project.
Copilot risk-analysis notes, including one example where you corrected or refined Copilot’s analysis.

title: Lab 4: AI Risk Analysis and Production Readiness
text:
7 Submission Instructions

title: Final Project Milestone 1: Proposal & Architecture
text:
Course Name/Number: COMP 331: AI-Assisted Software Engineering
Week: 10
Points: 80
This milestone builds on Labs 1-5, especially Lab 5: Coding Agents and Controlled Workflows.

title: Final Project Milestone 1: Proposal & Architecture
text:
1 Purpose of Assignment
This assignment is intended to help you:

Define the scope of your Controlled Coding Agent
Design a clear system architecture based on Labs 1-5
Establish a structured AI-assisted workflow
Create a realistic development plan for completion

title: Final Project Milestone 1: Proposal & Architecture
text:
2 Steps / Requirements of Assignment
Prepare a proposal and architecture plan for your final Controlled Coding Agent system. You may continue your lab repository or create a dedicated final project repository.

Define the problem your coding agent addresses.
Describe intended users and expected workflow.
Create an architecture diagram or structured architecture description.
Identify core components, including input, generation, evaluation, workflow, and persistence.
Create a development plan for Weeks 10-12.

title: Final Project Milestone 1: Proposal & Architecture
text:
3 Using GitHub Copilot
Use GitHub Copilot as a planning and critique partner, not as a substitute for your architecture decisions. You remain responsible for scope, feasibility, and tradeoffs.
Recommended Copilot workflow:

Ask Copilot Chat to critique your project scope for feasibility in the remaining course timeline.
Ask Copilot to generate two or three architecture alternatives, then choose and justify one.
Ask Copilot for likely risks, evaluation strategy ideas, and a milestone breakdown.
Document at least one Copilot-generated alternative that you did not choose and explain why.

title: Final Project Milestone 1: Proposal & Architecture
text:
4 Action Items

Click the link to the full hosted milestone instructions: Final Project Milestone 1.
Follow the instructions in the milestone page.
Go to github.com and either continue your lab repository or create a dedicated final project repository.
Create the proposal, architecture documentation or diagram, development plan, and milestone checklist described in the linked instructions.
Include AI-assisted planning notes showing Copilot prompts, alternatives considered, accepted ideas, rejected ideas, and your final decision.
Submit the repository link and paths to the proposal and architecture documentation through Canvas.

title: Final Project Milestone 1: Proposal & Architecture
text:
5 Deliverables
Your repository should include:

Project proposal.
Architecture documentation or diagram.
Development plan and milestone checklist.
AI-assisted planning notes showing at least one Copilot-generated alternative and your decision.
Initial implementation artifacts if available.

title: Gemini API Setup Guide
text: This guide supports Lab 1: Structured AI Workflow, where you will build the first version of a Java command-line program that sends a prompt to an LLM and prints the response.

title: Gemini API Setup Guide
text:
Large Language Models
Large language models, or LLMs, are the AI technology behind tools such as ChatGPT, Claude, Cursor, and Google Gemini. In this course, we will treat an LLM as a text-based service: your program sends a prompt, and the model returns a generated response.
We will use Google’s Gemini API to power the coding agent you build in Java. Gemini gives us a practical API for experimenting with prompt handling, model responses, tool use, and agent workflows.

title: Gemini API Setup Guide
text:
Cost and Rate Limits
Gemini API usage is subject to request and token limits. For this course, use only free-tier access unless your instructor gives different institutional guidance. Do not create a billing account or enable paid API usage for coursework. The course examples are designed to require only a small number of calls.
Every API call counts toward your usage, including calls made while testing your code. If you reach a quota or rate limit, wait for the quota window to reset, reduce how often your code calls the model, or ask your instructor whether the course has switched to an approved fallback provider. Creating a new API key should not be treated as a way to bypass quota limits.

title: Gemini API Setup Guide
text:
Tokens
Tokens are the units LLM APIs use to measure text input and output. A token is often a short word, part of a word, punctuation mark, or whitespace sequence. As a rough estimate, one token is about four characters for many English-language inputs, although the exact count varies by model and text.
Your usage in this course should be modest, but you should still monitor tokens and requests. Later labs ask you to add verbose or debug output so you can inspect model usage when the API provides that information.

title: Gemini API Setup Guide
text:
If Gemini Is Unavailable
Free API tiers change frequently. If Gemini becomes unreliable for course use, your instructor may announce an approved fallback provider. Do not switch providers on your own for graded work unless the assignment allows it, because request formats, model behavior, rate limits, and tool-calling support differ across APIs.
Possible free-tier or no-cost options your instructor may evaluate include:

GitHub Models: useful for experimentation if students already have GitHub access, but it does not provide a separate “GitHub Models API key” in the same way Gemini does. API access, when available, uses GitHub authentication such as a personal access token or a GitHub-provided token, and model availability and limits depend on GitHub’s current terms.
GroqCloud: often provides free developer access to fast open-weight models; limits and available models can change.
OpenRouter: sometimes offers free models through a single API; individual model availability varies.
Hugging Face Inference Providers: useful for experimenting with open models; free access may be limited by provider, account, and model.
Cloudflare Workers AI: may be practical when the course wants students to use a Cloudflare account; setup is different from a simple local API key.
Local models with Ollama or LM Studio: avoids API keys and cloud quotas, but requires enough laptop memory and may produce weaker or slower results.

For this course, the safest fallback design is to keep your Java code modular: isolate the model call behind a small class or interface so the rest of your agent does not depend on Gemini-specific request code.

title: Gemini API Setup Guide
text:
Create a Gemini API Key

Go to Google AI Studio.
Sign in with a Google account.
Click Get API key or Create API key.
If you already have a Google Cloud project, you may create the key in that project. Otherwise, AI Studio will guide you through creating a project.
Copy the API key. You will use it in your Java project.

If you get stuck, use the Gemini API key documentation.

title: Gemini API Setup Guide
text:
Store the API Key Safely
In your Lab 1 repository, create a file named .env in the project root:
GEMINI_API_KEY=your_api_key_here
Add .env to your .gitignore file:
.env
Never commit API keys, passwords, access tokens, or other secrets to Git. If you accidentally commit a key, revoke it immediately in Google AI Studio and create a new one.

title: Gemini API Setup Guide
Gemini API Setup Guide
Gemini API Setup Guide
text:
Run the Connection Check
Run your Java program from the project root. The exact command depends on your Maven setup. Common options include:
mvn compile
mvn exec:java
If everything is working, you should see Gemini’s response printed in your terminal. After this works, continue Lab 1 by replacing the hard-coded prompt with command-line argument handling and adding verbose/debug output.

title: Gemini API Setup Guide
text:
Troubleshooting

GEMINI_API_KEY was not found: confirm .env is in the project root and contains GEMINI_API_KEY=..., or set the variable in your shell.
403 or permission errors: confirm the API key is active and copied correctly.
429 or quota errors: you may have reached the free-tier limit. Wait for quota reset, reduce testing frequency, or ask whether an instructor-approved fallback is available.
JSON parsing errors: print the raw response body temporarily to inspect the returned shape. Do not print API keys.
Repeated testing consumes quota. Avoid running loops that call Gemini many times while debugging.

title: Syllabus: COMP 331: AI-Assisted Software Engineering
text:
This syllabus is referenced in the Course Orientation and provides the full course structure, learning outcomes, topics, grading, and weekly schedule. For tool installation and setup, see the Tool Setup Guide.
Course Name/Number: COMP 331: AI-Assisted Software Engineering
Format: 12-week
Credits: 4
Prerequisite: COMP 311 – Advanced Java Programming or equivalent programming experience

title: Syllabus: COMP 331: AI-Assisted Software Engineering
text:
Course Description
This course explores how software engineers can use AI-assisted tools intentionally while building controlled, evaluable coding-agent workflows. Students will use GitHub Copilot, Java, GitHub, testing tools, evaluation criteria, and structured documentation to move from basic AI-assisted coding toward a complete Controlled Coding Agent final project. Emphasis is placed on structured task input, eval-driven development, human oversight, risk analysis, reproducibility, and critically evaluating AI-generated outputs.

title: Syllabus: COMP 331: AI-Assisted Software Engineering
text:
Learning Outcomes
By the end of the course, students will be able to: - Set up and verify a Java development environment with GitHub Copilot and GitHub-based submission workflows. (Apply) - Document AI-assisted development decisions through weekly interaction logs. (Analyze) - Translate feature requests into structured task specifications for AI-assisted implementation. (Create) - Define and run evaluations that measure correctness and detect regressions in AI-generated outputs. (Evaluate) - Integrate structured input, AI-assisted generation, evaluation, and human approval into a controlled coding-agent workflow. (Create) - Analyze reliability, security, maintainability, and production-readiness risks in AI-assisted systems. (Analyze / Evaluate) - Deliver a complete Controlled Coding Agent final project with documentation, evaluation evidence, and a reproducible demo. (Create)

title: Syllabus: COMP 331: AI-Assisted Software Engineering
text:
Topics Covered

Environment setup and AI-assisted software engineering foundations
GitHub Copilot, GitHub Education, Git, Java IDEs, and repository-based submission workflows
Weekly AI interaction logging and evidence-based reflection
Structured AI workflows and task specifications
Eval-driven development, measurable correctness criteria, and regression detection
Integrated AI system implementation with structured input, generation, and evaluation
Risk analysis, reliability, security, maintainability, and production readiness
Controlled coding-agent workflows with human oversight checkpoints
Final Project proposal, architecture, partial implementation, evaluation strategy, complete system, and demo

title: Syllabus: COMP 331: AI-Assisted Software Engineering
text:
Textbooks & Materials

Selected academic journal and conference papers on AI in software engineering
Coursera specialization: Generative AI for Software Engineering
Official GitHub Copilot documentation and tutorials
Practical guides on prompt engineering and AI tooling focused on Copilot
Supplementary readings on evaluation, software testing, reproducibility, responsible AI, and software security

title: Syllabus: COMP 331: AI-Assisted Software Engineering
text:
Tools and Software
See the Tool Setup Guide for installation and configuration walkthroughs.

GitHub Copilot (primary AI tool integrated into Java IDEs). Students should set up GitHub Education with their university email address at github.com/settings/education/benefits to access education benefits, including Copilot access when approved.
Visual Studio Code or IntelliJ IDEA with GitHub Copilot plugin installed
Latest JDK
Git and GitHub for version control and project collaboration
JUnit 5 for Java unit testing
Maven or Gradle for build and dependency management

Assignments are weighted to build progressively from setup and weekly reflection into labs, controlled coding-agent workflows, and a complete final project.


Submission Expectations

Submit repository links, Canvas links, commit hashes, screenshots, reports, or demo links as requested in each assignment.
Maintain AI usage evidence, including prompts, Copilot interactions, accepted or rejected outputs, evaluations, and human decisions.
Keep README files current with setup, run, evaluation, and limitations information.
The Weekly AI Interaction Log is submitted weekly from Week 2 through Week 10.
Final Project submissions must be reproducible from a clean environment and include evaluation evidence.
